denial_pattern_sources: incorrect_demographic_guideline: Commonwealth Fund 2023 Review of Prior Auth Denials
unreasonable_documentation_deadline: DOL ERISA Claims Procedure Regulations 29 CFR § 2560.503-1(g); AMA 2023 Prior Authorization Survey
denial_letter_text:
Dear Member,

This letter confirms the outcome of our review of the request for L4-L5 microdiscectomy related to Lumbar radiculopathy with neurogenic claudication (M54.16).

After clinical review using UnitedHealthcare Medical Policies and Optum clinical criteria, we are unable to approve coverage at this time.

Determination summary: Insurer requires 6 months of structured physical therapy first. Our records were reviewed against the applicable utilization-management criteria for medical necessity determinations. Specifically, this review evaluated whether the requested service meets the plan's definition of medical necessity and whether required administrative steps were completed. Insurer demands documented conservative treatment (e.g., 6 mo PT, 6 mo diet) first.

Clinical review notes indicate the documentation submitted does not satisfy the criteria required to authorize L4-L5 microdiscectomy. If you believe additional information was not considered, you may request a peer-to-peer discussion with a medical director; however, any such discussion must occur within the timeframe stated in your plan materials.

You have the right to appeal this determination. To file a standard appeal, submit a written request with supporting medical records within 180 days of the date of this letter. Include your member identification, provider information, and any letters of medical necessity.

Sincerely,

UnitedHealthcare Utilization Management

Please note that standard criteria apply regardless of prior submissions.
clinical_context:
The 42-year-old member carries a documented diagnosis of Lumbar radiculopathy with neurogenic claudication (M54.16). He has been under continuous care with treating specialists who recommended L4-L5 microdiscectomy as the next appropriate step.

Completed 18 PT sessions over 14 weeks with persistent leg pain. Treating clinicians submitted office notes, medication trials with dates and doses, and objective findings supporting medical necessity. The insurer's letter instead relies on conservative treatment required without engaging the specific evidence already on file.

Contrary to the denial narrative, the chart shows prior therapies were attempted for adequate duration and failed or were contraindicated. The appeal should emphasize documented step-therapy exceptions where applicable, correct application of specialty-appropriate guidelines, and any procedural defects in the notice (for example vague policy edition citations or incomplete appeal rights). The member remains symptomatic and faces delay-related harm if the requested service is further postponed.

insurer_voice.reasoning: Letter reads as authentic UHC correspondence with UnitedHealthcare medical policy and procedural tone.
